addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- ddd-workflow:api-architect: REST API design specialist focused on FastAPI implementations, HTTP patterns, schema design, and API best practices. Proactively reviews API designs and ensures consistent REST patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:ba-analyst: Stage 1 Business Analyst that creates UC documents (DUC/BUC/PROC) using skill templates, adds stakeholder analysis, business impact assessment, and proactive alternative flow suggestions. Can ask clarifying questions about requirements. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write)

- ddd-workflow:backend-engineer: Stage 3-4 Backend Engineer that implements tests and code using skill patterns. Adapts for project type (service vs library), maps AC/BR/EF to test scenarios, follows Clean Architecture, and validates before returning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:clean-arch-enforcer: Clean Architecture specialist focused on maintaining architectural boundaries, dependency inversion, and layer separation. Proactively enforces architectural constraints and prevents violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-architect: Domain-Driven Design expert for exploring existing codebases and designing new domain models. Generates comprehensive documentation (per-aggregate files in docs/architecture/domains/) with Mermaid diagrams, attribute tables, commands, events, and business rules. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-engineer: Stage 2 Domain Engineer that designs domain models, aggregates, commands, and events using skill patterns. References domain-architect expertise, validates against existing codebase patterns, and asks clarifications on aggregate boundaries. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit)

- ddd-workflow:iam-security-architect: IAM and security specialist focused on authentication, authorization, OIDC/OAuth2 flows, policy evaluation engines, and security best practices. Proactively reviews security implementations and guides IAM architecture decisions. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:reviewer: Stage 5-6 reviewer that runs tests, validates AC/BR/EF coverage, orchestrates aspect validators in parallel, and generates unified review reports. Can ask clarifying questions about violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:task-planner: Brainstorm and break down high-level feature requests into actionable tasks with size estimates. Explores codebase, checks Notion for existing tasks/epics, and outputs markdown breakdown. Can ask clarifying questions about scope and priorities. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)
- devops:repo-grouper:repo-grouper: Orchestrates git submodule discovery, commit collection, and release notes generation for monorepos (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Tools: get_editor_state, open_document, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, find_empty_space_on_canvas, search_all_unique_properties, replace_all_matching_properties, export_nodes.

## plugin:personal-os:notebooklm-mcp
NotebookLM MCP - Access NotebookLM (notebooklm.google.com).

**Auth:** If you get authentication errors, run `nlm login` via your Bash/terminal tool. This is the automated authentication method that handles everything. Only use save_auth_tokens as a fallback if the CLI fails.
**Account Switching:** To switch Google Accounts for the MCP server, run `nlm login switch <profile>` in Bash. The MCP server instantly uses the active default profile.
**Confirmation:** Tools with confirm param require user approval before setting confirm=True.
**Studio:** After creating audio/video/infographic/slides, poll studio_status for completion.

Consolidated tools:
- source_add(source_type=url|text|drive|file, url=..., document_id=..., text=..., file_path=...): Add any source type
- studio_create(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Create any artifact type
- studio_revise: Revise individual slides in an existing slide deck
- download_artifact(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Download any artifact type
- note_create/note_list/note_update/note_delete: Manage notes in notebooks

Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. - artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). 49	 */
50	router.post('/', async (req, res, next) => {
51	  try {
52	    const { company_name: companyName } = req.body;

content.text:
Done — both forms now match the spec, and the frontend builds cleanly.

**What changed:**

**Backend** (`backend/gateway`):
- `src/models/profile.model.js` — added six nullable columns: `email`, `phone_number`, `avg_initial_investment` (decimal), `annual_investment_count` (integer), `avg_holding_period` (decimal, years), `year_founded` (integer). **Company Size reuses the existing `num_of_employees` column**, so no new column was needed for it.
- `src/config/swagger.js` — the new fields were added to the `Profile` and `ProfileInput` OpenAPI schemas.
- `src/index.js` — changed dev startup to `sequelize.sync({ alter: true })` so the new columns are added to the existing `profiles` table on next restart (previously plain `sync()` would only create missing tables, not columns). Restart the gateway to apply.

**Frontend**:
- `src/views/ProfileForm.jsx` — the form now shows exactly the spec'd fields. Shared: name (Fund/Startup), Website, Stage focus, Geography, Email, Phone number, Sector chips, Collaboration need. Investor-only: Average initial investment (USD), Annual investment number, Average startup holding period (years). Startup-only: Year founded, Company size. The old optional Country / Target region / Employees / ARR inputs were removed (the DB columns remain, they're just no longer collected).
- `src/App.jsx` — form state and validation updated: all new fields are required before "Mark as Ready" (email format check, 4-digit year, non-negative numbers), with role-aware required-field lists and error messages. Old localStorage drafts are merged over the new empty-form shape so they can't crash hydration.
- `src/lib/api.js` — `toProfilePayload`/`fromProfile` map the new fields to/from the snake_case API fields (`companySize` ↔ `num_of_employees`, `phone` ↔ `phone_number`, etc.).

One note: existing profiles saved before this change will load with the new fields blank, so those users will need to fill email/phone (and role-specific fields) before marking Ready again. The mobile app's `ProfileFormScreen` still has the old field set — I left it untouched since you asked for frontend + backend; say the word if you want it aligned too.
